nodes.title: [2023-01-31] Complete miscarriage diagnosed using ultrasound or pelvic exam
nodes.description: A complete miscarriage is typically diagnosed using an ultrasound or pelvic exam to confirm that the uterus is empty, and there are no products of conception remaining.

nodes.title: [2023-01-31] Blood tests check pregnancy hormone levels after complete miscarriage
nodes.description: Blood tests may also be done to check the levels of pregnancy hormones in the body, which will typically drop significantly after a complete miscarriage.

nodes.title: [2023-01-31] Potential risks associated with complete miscarriage include infection
nodes.description: There are a few potential risks associated with a complete miscarriage, including infection: in some cases, tissue left behind after a miscarriage can lead to infection, which can be treated with antibiotics.

nodes.title: [2023-01-31] Potential risks associated with complete miscarriage include heavy bleeding
nodes.description: There are a few potential risks associated with a complete miscarriage, including heavy bleeding: while some bleeding is normal after a miscarriage, heavy bleeding (like soaking through a pad in an hour or less) may require medical attention.

nodes.title: [2023-01-31] Potential risks associated with complete miscarriage include emotional effects
nodes.description: There are a few potential risks associated with a complete miscarriage, including emotional effects: miscarriage can be emotionally challenging for many women and their partners, and may require support from friends, family, or a mental health professional.

nodes.title: [2023-01-31] Potential risks associated with complete miscarriage include future pregnancy complications
nodes.description: There are a few potential risks associated with a complete miscarriage, including future pregnancy complications: while a complete miscarriage does not typically affect a woman's ability to get pregnant in the future, there may be some increased risk of complications in future pregnancies, particularly if the miscarriage was caused by an underlying condition like endometriosis or hormonal imbalances.

nodes.title: [2023-01-31] Complete miscarriage can happen without symptoms, called missed miscarriage
nodes.description: A complete miscarriage can happen without any symptoms or noticeable bleeding and cramping; this is called a missed miscarriage.

nodes.title: [2023-01-31] Missed miscarriage diagnosed during routine prenatal checkup or ultrasound
nodes.description: A missed miscarriage is often diagnosed during a routine prenatal checkup or ultrasound, where no fetal heartbeat is detected, or the fetus appears smaller than expected.

nodes.title: [2023-01-31] Complete miscarriage can occur with symptoms like bleeding and cramping
nodes.description: A complete miscarriage can also occur with symptoms, which typically include bleeding, cramping, and the passing of tissue from the uterus.

nodes.title: [2023-01-31] Common causes of complete miscarriage include chromosomal abnormalities
nodes.description: The exact cause of a miscarriage is often unknown, but it may occur due to chromosomal abnormalities, hormonal imbalances, or problems with the uterus or cervix.

nodes.title: [2023-01-31] Factors increasing risk of miscarriage include age and smoking
nodes.description: Other factors that can increase the risk of miscarriage include age, smoking, drug and alcohol use, certain chronic medical conditions like diabetes, and infections.

nodes.title: [2023-01-31] Excessive physical activity is not a common cause of complete miscarriage
nodes.description: In general, excessive physical activity or lifting heavy objects is not considered a common cause of a complete miscarriage.

nodes.title: [2023-01-31] Most miscarriages occur due to genetic abnormalities and health conditions
nodes.description: While there are exceptions, most miscarriages occur due to genetic abnormalities, problems with the placenta, infections or health conditions, rather than specific physical activities.

nodes.title: [2023-01-31] Physical trauma may cause miscarriage in rare cases
nodes.description: In rare cases, physical trauma or injury may cause a miscarriage; however, this usually involves a significant impact, such as a car accident, rather than everyday activities like lifting heavy objects or exercising.

nodes.title: [2023-01-31] Some medications can cause complete miscarriage, especially in first trimester
nodes.description: Some medications can cause a complete miscarriage, particularly if they are taken during the first trimester of pregnancy when the risk of miscarriage is highest.

nodes.title: [2023-01-31] Misoprostol and mifepristone used to induce miscarriage
nodes.description: Certain drugs, like misoprostol and mifepristone, are sometimes used medically to induce a miscarriage when necessary, such as in cases of incomplete miscarriage or when the pregnancy is not viable.

nodes.title: [2023-01-31] Taking birth control pills while pregnant is not recommended
nodes.description: Taking birth control pills while pregnant is not recommended, as they are designed to prevent pregnancy and could potentially harm a developing fetus.

nodes.title: [2023-01-31] Rare for pregnancy to occur while taking birth control pills as directed
nodes.description: It is important to note that it is rare for a pregnancy to occur while taking birth control pills as directed.

nodes.title: [2023-01-31] Stress is unlikely to directly cause complete miscarriage
nodes.description: While stress can have negative effects on pregnancy, it is unlikely to directly cause a complete miscarriage.

nodes.title: [2023-01-31] Limited evidence linking stress to miscarriage
nodes.description: There is limited evidence linking stress to miscarriage; stressful events may increase the risk of miscarriage or other pregnancy complications, but the evidence is not conclusive.

nodes.title: [2023-01-31] Chronic stress can lead to unhealthy habits affecting pregnancy
nodes.description: Chronic stress can lead to an overproduction of stress hormones like cortisol, which can affect the immune system and lead to inflammation which could, in rare cases, contribute to miscarriage.

nodes.title: [2023-01-31] Moderate caffeine consumption during pregnancy is generally considered safe
nodes.description: While there is some debate and conflicting evidence, drinking moderate amounts of caffeine during pregnancy is generally considered safe and is unlikely to cause a complete miscarriage.

nodes.title: [2023-01-31] American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists recommends limiting caffeine intake
nodes.description: The American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists suggests that pregnant individuals limit their caffeine intake to less than 200 milligrams per day, which is roughly the amount found in one 12-ounce cup of coffee.

nodes.title: [2023-01-31] Excessive caffeine consumption associated with increased risk of miscarriage
nodes.description: However, excessive consumption of caffeine has been associated with a slightly increased risk of miscarriage, particularly in the first trimester when the risk of miscarriage is already highest.

nodes.title: [2023-01-31] Exposure to certain chemicals can increase risk of complete miscarriage
nodes.description: Exposure to certain chemicals can increase the risk of a complete miscarriage, although the exact chemicals and levels of exposure needed to cause a miscarriage are not always clear.

nodes.title: [2023-01-31] Pregnant women should limit exposure to chemicals
nodes.description: Pregnant women should try to limit their exposure to chemicals as much as possible, including avoiding cigarette smoking, drinking alcohol, and using illicit drugs, which are well-known risks for pregnancy complications.

nodes.title: [2023-01-31] Pregnant women should avoid exposure to pesticides and harsh cleaning products
nodes.description: To help protect their pregnancy, pregnant women should also avoid exposure to pesticides, insecticides, and other toxins from chemicals in the workplace, and avoid using harsh cleaning products that contain chemicals like ammonia, bleach or solvents.

nodes.title: [2023-01-31] Pregnant women should avoid exposure to heavy metals like lead and mercury
nodes.description: Pregnant women should also avoid exposure to heavy metals such as lead and mercury that are found in some fish, some types of pottery, and certain industrial exposures.

nodes.title: [2023-01-31] Pregnant women should use natural or organic personal care products
nodes.description: Pregnant women should use natural or organic personal care products, such as shampoos, soaps, and lotions, which contain fewer synthetic chemicals than conventional products.
